author: @davidjackson2115
text: Thanks to some jig or delenquent  'life that matters'  who thought it funny to shot my ground standing panel with an air gun =  burnt  "square"  helped confuse the cause.  Ive only learned today that the cells only put out 0.5 volts and are all connected in series.  I see you can get the cells of aliexpress.  Given storm or war damage of panels what would you suggest.  As all I can think is to cut the connections of damaged cell and solder new one on top {likely drilling or grinding to get clear connection}  and clear coat / varnish under and over for gluing and sealing.  And even then you really want an epoxy to avoid shrinkage and cracking of the delecate crystals.  Yours was a nice easy repair skirting around the edges.  What would you suggest for the deeper stuff.  And um  BLM  remember,  helps employ security guards / fencing /  police to chase tail /  victims to feel sorry for themselves etc.....

author: @nicgreen4234
text: Good repair on the open circuit Rob. The most common fault I come across is blown diodes, low current being a pointer to this. Their a cheap component and typically a tool free fix. Might be worth checking on your panel.

author: @lowrads
text:
It's kinds neat that PV cells can still work even when they are broken, and that one can make custom output panels just by combining cells into strings.

Looks like this panel could benefit from a few diodes added to the strings.

author: @deslomeslager
text:
Don't try the drone motor on this one!
PS: I used a knife blade and scratched off a bit of the back side to access the metal. My aim was to cut the series in 2 series (in parallel) with a lower voltage. From 38 Volt to ~ 19 Volt.

author: @BrianKeithPREPPER
text: What is xylene, where to get it and what not? I've never used it before. Thx

replies.author: @AndyFletcherX31
replies.text: It is thinner/brush cleaner for hammerite paint

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: Use a chemical mask when using it. It is some nasty stuff.

replies.author: @edwarddiviney5226
replies.text: Many years ago I worked for the company that pioneered "Fracking" in the oil fields, some of the wells we serviced were thick with parafin, we used 28% HCl acid and or 100% xylene to help dissolve it. I got a few drops on me, burns like hell, until washed off. Extremely flammable and ate right through some of our fluid valves if not diluted. Valves in storage tank were a different material.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: xylene doesn't burn like hell - even 100% - it's a bit cold and mostly sold as paint thinner

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a kind of paint thinner mate - you can buy it online easily enough
